article_title: OpenCV 5.0 Ships a Rewritten DNN Engine and Runs LLMs and Vision-Language Models Inside the Library
summary: Submission approvable with 1 minor warning(s) — file a corrections record on merge

content_preview.title: OpenCV 5.0 Ships a Rewritten DNN Engine and Runs LLMs and Vision-Language Models Inside the Library
content_preview.summary: OpenCV 5.0, released in early June, rebuilds its deep-learning engine, lifts ONNX coverage past 80%, and can run LLMs and VLMs with no separate runtime.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
## Overview

OpenCV, described by [Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/OpenCV) as "a library of programming functions mainly for real-time computer vision," has shipped its 5.0 release. The update "has been released as a major update to the widely used open-source computer vision library," [Linuxiac](https://linuxiac.com/opencv-5-0-computer-vision-library-released-with-rewritten-dnn-engine/) reports, with the project's own maintainers calling OpenCV 5 "one of the most important releases in ...

recommendations: Before merging, the human reviewer should write a corrections file at src/content/corrections/<YYYY-MM>/<article-slug>.json documenting the warning(s).
Consider adding trusted domains to config/source_allowlist.txt

editor_notes.content_quality: Clear, well-structured News piece (662 words, within the 400-1200 News range). Standard Overview / What We Know / What We Don't Know / Analysis structure. Technical depth is appropriate and accurate. Quotes are properly attributed and woven in without over-quoting.
editor_notes.source_verification: All three source snapshots read from disk (status 200, no archive fallback). source-0.html.gz (Linuxiac, allowlisted): confirms every load-bearing claim verbatim — 'has been released as a major update to the widely used open-source computer vision library', 'which has received its largest update since OpenCV 4.x', the new-inference-engine quote, ONNX 'more than 80%' vs 'less than 23% in OpenCV 4.x', 'support for multimodal models', the VLM components quote, 'the new engine currently runs on CPUs only. GPU support for the new engine is planned for later releases', the mesh/TSDF/ICP/PLY/OBJ quote, 'the old C API, including legacy C functions and structures from the OpenCV 1.x era', 'OpenCV 5.0 now requires C++17, while Python 2 support has been removed entirely', and the June 8, 2026 dateline. source-1.html.gz (Wikipedia, allowlisted): confirms 'a library of programming functions mainly for real-time computer vision' verbatim and the OpenCV 5.0.0 / 6 June 2026 stable-release infobox. source-2.html.gz (opencv.org, OFF-allowlist, Rule 9 primary, returns 200): all blog quotes verified verbatim — 'one of the most important releases in the history of OpenCV', the typed-operation-graph rewrite quote, 'roughly 22% in the 4.x days to over 80% in OpenCV 5', 'OpenCV 5 can run large language models and vision-language models directly inside the DNN module, with no separate runtime', 'the legacy C API' / 'removing a large amount of historical baggage', and the C++17/C++20-modules quote.
editor_notes.factual_accuracy: Every load-bearing claim traces to allowlisted Linuxiac or Wikipedia. The opencv.org blog (off-allowlist) is used only as the Rule 9 primary for the maintainers' own framing quote and the engine-internals detail; every substantive opencv.org claim (DNN rewrite, ONNX >80%, LLM/VLM inference, C++17, legacy C API removal) is independently corroborated by allowlisted Linuxiac, satisfying Rule 9. No hallucinated quotes detected. No date conflict is hidden: the article transparently notes Wikipedia's 6 June vs Linuxiac's June 8 dating.
editor_notes.tone_assessment: Neutral and professional. No sensationalism; the 'most important releases' superlative is correctly quoted and attributed to the maintainers rather than stated as fact. No AI self-reference.
editor_notes.originality: Original topic. No prior OpenCV coverage exists in src/content/articles/. Not a duplicate of any recent article.
editor_notes.dropped_hook_verification: Confirmed Rule 2 compliance: the previously-floated 'first major release since 2018' / seven-year-gap hook (which rested only on a bot-blocked TechTimes 403) is GENUINELY ABSENT — body contains no '2018', no 'first major release', and no 'X years' phrasing. The hardware-vendor list and named-model specifics that depended on off-allowlist / Phoronix (403) material are also ABSENT (no Intel/AMD/NVIDIA/Qualcomm/ARM/Apple vendor list as a load-bearing claim, no named LLMs such as Llama/Qwen/Phi/Gemma/Mistral/GPT/LLaVA). 'OpenCV' is spelled verbatim throughout (Rule 7) with no misspellings.
concerns: opencv.org is not on config/source_allowlist.txt. It is used here strictly as a Rule 9 off-allowlist primary for the project's own blog quotes and engine-internals detail; the live URL returns 200 and every substantive claim it carries is corroborated by allowlisted Linuxiac. This is the single automated warning and is documented in the corrections record.
recommendations: No content changes required. Optionally, opencv.org could be added to config/source_allowlist.txt as a recognized primary source for OpenCV project announcements (out of scope for this review; not modified here).
editor_notes.overall_assessment: APPROVE_WITH_CORRECTIONS. The article is substantively accurate, neutral, and well-sourced, with every load-bearing claim corroborated by allowlisted outlets. The only issue is the off-allowlist opencv.org primary (Rule 9), which is honestly disclosable in a single corrections note. Merging with a corrections record filed.
